narrative_summary:
The BRCA2 c.8702G>A (p.Gly2901Asp) missense change occurs in exon 21 of the DNA‑binding domain of BRCA2 and has been investigated in relation to hereditary breast and ovarian cancer risk. Early reports described segregation of the variant with ovarian cancer in three sisters and its absence in 311 unrelated patients and 220 cancer‑free controls, suggesting a possible pathogenic role [PMC2150153]. Subsequent case‑control and cohort studies from Asian populations have provided divergent clinical interpretations: a Malaysian cohort listed the variant among deleterious mutations [PMC2575532], whereas studies of Hakka ovarian cancer patients, Chinese Hakka breast/ovarian cohorts, Taiwanese and broader Chinese populations classified it as likely benign or benign [PMC8922037, PMC9347172, PMC8256441, PMC6617753, PMC10988807].

Functional assays consistently indicate that the Gly2901Asp substitution does not impair BRCA2 activity. Mouse embryonic stem‑cell rescue experiments showed no loss of cell viability or increased DNA‑damage sensitivity, leading the authors to conclude the variant is neutral [PMC2640324]. Multiplexed mouse ESC functional screens reported probability‑of‑impact‑on‑function confidence intervals compatible with a neutral effect [PMC10694496], and homology‑directed repair (HDR) assays yielded neutral scores (HDR classification “Neutral”) and supported a benign interpretation within an ACMG/AMP framework [PMC9433957, PMC8008494].

Computational predictions uniformly flag the change as deleterious: PolyPhen‑2 scores near 1.0, SIFT scores of 0, high Grantham distances, and protein‑likelihood ratios >5 have been reported, leading to a computational classification of likely pathogenic in several studies [PMC5936051, PMC8670232, PMC4519264, PMC2587343]. Population data show the variant is rare (allele frequencies on the order of 0.0001–0.00013) and observed primarily in Asian cohorts, but rarity alone has not been taken as evidence of pathogenicity [PMC8670232, PMC8125125].

The evidence is therefore discordant: early segregation data and in‑silico tools suggest pathogenicity, whereas multiple functional assays and several clinical classification efforts support a benign or neutral effect. Given the weight of experimental functional data and the prevailing clinical reclassifications toward benignity, the variant is best regarded as likely benign/neutral, with residual uncertainty stemming from limited segregation data and conflicting computational predictions.

mentions.quoted_text: In the functional analysis of the 78 VUSs, 9 mutations were predicted to be deleterious by PolyPhen-2 and SIFT. All of them were evaluated by PhyloP and Grantham for evolutionary conservation. According to the BIC database, the only reports of mutations c.5504G>A and **c.8702G>A** were in 2 Malaysians and 3 Asians, respectively. This suggests that these 2 mutations may be specific to Asian populations. Moreover, mutations c.80G>A, c.733G>T, c.8574A>T and c.7857G>C were novel, as they have never been reported before. Notably, the BRCA1 protein harbors an N-terminal RING-finger domain characteristic of ubiquitin E3 ligases, which is associated with tumor suppression. The mutation c.80G>A (Cys27Tyr) is located in the RING-finger domain and removes the second cysteine of the putative C3HC4 zinc-binding motif, which would be expected to have a significant effect on the DNA binding properties of the BRCA1 protein.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study identifies BRCA2 c.8702G>A (p.Gly2901Asp) as a variant of uncertain significance, predicted to be deleterious by in‑silico tools, with no additional clinical or functional evidence presented.
article_mentions.title: Prevalence and Spectrum of BRCA1/2 Germline Mutations in Women with Breast Cancer in China Based on Next-Generation Sequencing

mentions.quoted_text: Notably, three BRCA2 variants (p.Ala1996Thr, **p.Gly2901Asp** and p.Tyr3035Cys) which are predicted to be pathogenic by in-silico prediction models were found in cases and in healthy controls. The clinical significance of BRCA2 p.Ala1996Thr is currently listed as uncertain and the substitution of valine at the same codon (p.Ala1996Val) in a Western European woman is also uncertain. BRCA2 **p.Gly2901Asp** was suggested as neutral in mouse embryonic stem cell-based functional assay, and predicted to be uncertain in protein likelihood ratios and homology-directed repair activity. Although BRCA2 p.Tyr3035Cys was predicted to be likely deleterious in protein likelihood ratios, this variant did not show any significant association with breast cancer risk in our study.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study reports multiplexed mouse ESC functional assays and provides quantitative functional data (scores and PIF confidence intervals) for the BRCA2 G2901D variant, but does not explicitly state its clinical classification.
article_mentions.title: Sequencing-based functional assays for classification of BRCA2 variants in mouse ESCs

mentions.quoted_text: We first tested 7 BRCA2 variants (see Table 1), five of them (Y42C, D1420Y, V2728I, E2856A, and S2988G) with a limited evolutionary conservation and two (E2663V, **G2901D**) conserved across the animal kingdom and plants. All less-conserved variants sustained the ES-cell viability after the endogenous Brca2 was deleted and did not show hypersensitivity to any DNA-damaging agents. Four of these mutations, with the exception of S2988G that was found only once, have been frequently recorded in the BIC database8 and epidemiological data (Table 1) strongly suggested that these were neutral variants. Despite these data, Y42C was shown by in vitro studies to disrupt an interaction between the N-terminus of BRCA2 and the replication protein A (RPA)9, a protein essential for DNA repair, which directly contradicts the epidemiological studies10. Lack of any deleterious effect of Y42C in ES cells further validates our experimental system and reveals limitations of experiments in which an isolated portion of the protein is used to draw conclusions about causality.

The pathogenic potential of two evolutionary conserved variants, **G2901D** and E2663V, was unknown (see Table 1). Our assay shows that **G2901D** has no effect on BRCA2 function (Supplementary Table 1 online), suggesting that this mutation is neutral. The other variant, E2663V, did not rescue the cells after the Cre expression and, thus, should be classified as deleterious. Interestingly, it has recently been reported as a variant with high probability to be classified as deleterious11.

We have provided here a preliminary validation of our ES-cell-based functional assay for evaluating mutations in human BRCA2 by testing 17 variants. Although, the ultimate validation of this assay in a true epidemiological sense is not possible at present, for 13 variants (four truncating variants excluding E3309X, plus six missense variants Y42C, N991D, D1420Y, E2663V, D2723H, V2728H, E2856A, R3052Q and one missense mutation, T2722R, which also affects splicing) the results obtained in our assay completely agreed with predictions based on epidemiological studies and other available functional data6,7,13,24. The functional significance of 4 other mutations (E3309X, **G2901D**, S2988G, and R3052W) was unknown and reported here for the first time. Future studies will show how the range of phenotypes revealed for different mutations, i.e. cell lethality, degree of sensitivity to DNA damaging agents and, possibly, specific sensitivity to only a subset of toxic compounds correlates with their penetrance and disease presentation in humans.

Rows:
conforma- tion-sensitive gel electrophoresis (CSGE) (Ganguly et al, 1993). Samples showing variant migration patterns were reamplified and directly sequenced using fluorescent dye terminators and analysed on an ABI 377 DNA sequencer. Analysis of **G8702A** using a PCR restriction enzyme assay Screening of the G to A change at nucleotide 8702 was performed by restriction enzyme digestion. The PCR amplification was performed in a total volume of 50 gl, including 150 ng genomic DNA, 25 gM of dNTP
and derived from 26 different families (Auranen et al, 1996). Among the other 146 cases there was a series of 15 patients who were selected by age (younger than 40 years at the time of diag- nosis). The remaining 131 cases were unselected. The **G8702A** change was also searched for in index patient DNA samples for 100 Finnish breast cancer families (with three or more cases of breast or ovarian cancer in first- or second-degree relatives, all probands affected with breast cancer) previously
breast cancer only families and 15 breast-ovarian families were negative for both (Vehmanen et al, 1997). In addi- tion, 21 breast cancer families not scrutinized for the above criteria (not previously analysed for BRCA1 or BRCA2) were studied for **G8702A** variant. None of these 311 patients or 220 cancer-free controls showed the change. DISCUSSION We have detected a BRCA2 sequence variant that is predicted to generate a missense amino acid change in a family with three sisters affected by ovarian

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports that the BRCA2 G2901D missense variant segregates with ovarian cancer in three sisters and is absent in controls, indicating it is likely pathogenic for ovarian cancer susceptibility.
article_mentions.title: A missense mutation in the BRCA2 gene in three siblings with ovarian cancer.
